commentBody:
My five favorite original clues from last week
(in order of appearance):

1. Whom a diary is often shown to  (2)(3)
2. Sound-track?  (10)
3. [Taps casino table]  (3)(2)
4. Pressure gauge?  (6)(5)
5. ___ Story (punny nickname for the "Iliad")  (4)


NO ONE
ECHOLOCATE
HIT ME
STRESS LEVEL
TROY

Two SMÉAGOLS in three days.  There have only been two other appearances of this character ever.  The last one before Saturday's was in 2018.  So was the other one.  They appeared about two months apart.

Who'da thunk it?
What if the hokey pokey really *is* what it's all about?

Easy, even by Monday standards

3:12

Easy but fun puzzle today. Good way to start off the week!

Got confused with CHESSMOVE vs checkmate. Doesn't help that I'm not sure what APRS was. So had to wait until the end to rejig that section.

Pretty sure Deb Amlen would have gone with THEDOCTOR before DOCTORWHO. 

Hope you're enjoying the next phase if your life, Deb.
Am I going a bit crazy or is there a Midi crossword in the works?

A soothing Sunday afternoon solve, just what was needed at the close of a fraught week, SMEAGOL notwithstanding (never sure what that means, but I think it means what I meant).

Thank you, Leslie, for constructing this one, and congratulations on your debut. You seem properly obsessed, so I'm trusting we can count on you for more.

Fast solve and lots of fun! Unrelated but question — is the midi crossword only released for US located subscribers at the moment? I'm located in Australia, and cannot access the page at all (and it's not in my app despite making sure it's up to date!) When I tried to visit this URL, https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/archive/midi I get the error that "This page no longer exists"

Ok, very easy for a Monday. I had Ohey instead of OHMY but that was a quick fix. Not a huge chess player but knew enough to put in chessmove and was correct. Like everyone else amazed to see Smeagol clued twice in three days. However, this clue was more straightforward unless you wanted to go with Gollum first. 3:45 under my average over my best by 1:23

CONGRATULATIONS on your debut, Leslie. I thought that deserved a SHOUT OUT 😉 We saw TIMBER WOLves walking along the frozen Milwaukee River when we lived in Wisconsin. They were huge and way too close to our house. There was only one house across the street separating us from the river. 

Happy Monday Crosslandia.

Good job on the debut, Leslie! 😉 This puzzle flew by fast; a lot of the notes made above by NYT themselves didn't even cross my mind, I just filled them in fast! New record, by the way. And I also learned that Gollum was called SMEAGOL, so it's kinda odd. Keep it up, Leslie! 🥳

Leslie,
Nice debut!

Will, et al,
Can we get Mondays back up to NYT level by getting rid of the shading, starring the four themer clues, and having the revealer refer to the "starts" of the four themers?  Thanks.
For someone that’s having a pretty bad day, I found this puzzle to be nice and uplifting. Thanks and congrats on your debut, Leslie!

Hey, I just posted about the HOKEYPOKEY out here in the comments just a few days ago. NYT is stealing my intellectual property!! I want a cut in the deal!!  Har!

Actually, it was fun to see that show up! But I think it hasn't been included since 2009, so it is funny that it came up just a few days after I mentioned it. Believe it or not I don't actually mention the hokey pokey very often in my everyday life. 

Now the lambada, that I talk about all the time! Gotta love the forbidden dance! 💃🕺

Anyhow, nice theme, nice puzzle, nice debut. 

Somehow, without trying, it was my best Monday time--by far. I don't try to be fast, so it kind of surprised me... 

Anyhow, time to wind down, or maybe it's wind up, for the week ahead. Not sure which but please know there will be winding involved. Don't worry, I'll wear a helmet!

Said the director while filming the movie about mathematics, "Lights! Camera! Fractions!"

(The reviews were mixed...numbers.)
A lot of people in this chat forgetting that they already saw SMEAGOL just a few days ago. Gimme for me both times, though
Solid, pleasant Monday. Congratulations on your debut.

OKG OMG OMG 

MY FASTEST MONDAY EVER! Go 4:20! Perfect time for ARBOR day!!

Hope everyone else has a strong start to March!

Onward ho!!

3:23

New record, by a lot.

Great crossword today! I did in 14:16 using Across clues only, no Downs. Yay me! 

Great to see SMEAGOL making a comeback!
Short and sweet like a scoop of Hokey Pokey, National ice cream of New Zealand, unofficially. Boysenberry swirl is up there too.  Finally back on a streak after some Sunday and work delays.
New recorddddd lets gooo

Perfect Monday. Nothing more to say.
(Except: thanks Leslie!)
Congrats on the solid debut

When I was learning German in my teens, I often browsed Langenscheidt's German-Polish dictionary. I found it an uninteresting yet quite effective method of expanding my vocabulary. 

So there I am one day, opening the small yet thick book at random, memorizing word after word. It's going great. The dictionary has this rubberized cover, quite nice to the touch, and the thin yet surprisingly substantial pages make a fascinating, weirdly pleasant sound when I leaf through them, like baby asps zig-zagging across the reedy wetness of a papyrus thicket.

The snakelets stop in the middle of the Ms. An unfamiliar word pops at me, daring me to learn it. MOPED!

What could it possibly be? It sounds so alien! My mind stutters, confused. My eyes glide hungrily along the entry:

Moped [ˈmoːpɛt; -peːt] n

Yes, yes, but tell me what it means! I'm dying to know!

Ok then, the dictionary says, I'll tell you. Tiny forked tongues vibrate on the page. The anticipation is delicious.

It means...

Moped!

The betrayal! All this seduction - the yielding rubber of the cover, the hypnotic, reptilian rustle of the inviting pages - and then... Denial, rejection! I've been jilted by knowledge.

(The thing was, while moped *theoretically* is  a word in Polish, literally nobody ever uses it outside dictionaries, as we have another term for the thing: "motorower," literally "bicycle with an engine." Thus, the dictionary entry was the perfect example of the "ignotum per ignotum" logical conundrum)

@Melissa Kirsch
We share the same “knowledge” limitations.  Sports are in my wheel house, though.  I’ve got that going for me.

There are many instances of movies with nearly identical plots being released within months of each other. The Illusionist/The Prestige, Deep Impact/Armageddon, Antz/A Bug’s Life, the slew of body-switching movies in the late 1980s… how is it that so many creative people just happen to come up with the same ideas at the same time? This phenomenon, known as Twin Movies, is usually attributed to eavesdropping and industry spies. Which leads me to wonder…

Are there events where crossword constructors throw out their best clues for others to hear? Is there a network of puzzle moles who regularly hack into the NYT database? How to explain the Twin Puzzle phenomenon of dueling Sméagols, tandem Captain Phillips, and other duplicate entries appearing within days of each other?

I don’t know, but something tells me Yoko Ono is behind this.
I don't get the theme. Is it a golf thing?
acuter and arear were a little rough but arguably worth it for the theme

Well done Leslie.
Didn't achieve my No Look-Ups on a Monday goal because of the cake. I've never heard of tres leches, and it was hard to guess, wasn't really sure how to solve Yikes! or even Good Heavens! 
Slowed down a little by DIF, ARBOR day and PARADE and of course I play the Hokey Cokey!
BRAVO

In order that I might (no failsafe aspiration) reach lofty goals, I usually set them with a tempered disinclination toward over-confidence. The Midi inspired this latest conjecture: strive to solve the Mini quickerer than the new kid on the block, the embraceable Midi. 

The jury has the case.

Oh, I greatly enjoyed this theme.

I saw and heard the golfer and other shouters, which brought fun into the solve.

The shouts are not directly related to the answers they are embedded in.  FORESHADOW has nothing to do with golf, nor ACTION PLAN with movies, and so on.  That's a nice touch.

It may not be hard to come up with words that are often shouted, like “Sold!” or “Bingo!”, but finding bigger words that start with unrelated shout-words – not so easy.  

Coming up with four such answers that fit symmetry, well, that’s moving into wow territory.

Making them fresh is even more impressive.  The five theme answers include ia NYT debut, two once-befores, and two twice-befores.  Brava on that!

Finally, a serendipity in the grid greatly pleased nerdy me.  Near IDAHO is ATOP, and if you read ATOP from right to left, then continue in the other direction two more letters, it spells “potato”.  Hah!

Your puzzle sparked my day, Leslie.  Congratulations on your debut, and thank you!
Congratulations to today's constructor on the debut.  Nice puzzle, could have done without the shaded squares.  Enjoyed your comments too.

Where did they come up with that mysterious, alien word at 55A? Seems like they just pulled it out of their ‘Area R’.

A good puzzle - I didn’t scream once.

Really enjoyable Monday puzzle. A lot of working the crosses but everything fell together smoothly. And then... I didn't catch on to the theme until I worked out the reveal. That was just a nice 'aha' moment when I finally saw it. Looking forward to more from Ms. Benson. 

And I'll put my puzzle find today in a reply.

My usual clarification, before someone posts the usual misinformation: we are purportedly happy "at high tide" because that is when we are spared predation. No! All sorts of nasty predators come up from the sea and are actively hunting us at high tide. Crabs, fishes, moon snails, whelks, and so on. We are happy at high tide because we get to be active then -- for example, we get to eat! During low tide, we have to, well, CLAM up. We cannot feed, we are subjected to thermal (and sometimes salinity) stress, we often have to go anaerobic, and so on. We get to recover from all that stress when the mother ocean comes to cover us again. And that recovery takes energy, so we have to feed! Glorious, succulent phytoplankton! MMMmmMMMmmm! Soup's on!

Whoa! - one of the most amazing puzzles I've ever come across. A Sunday from May 27, 2018 by Andrew Chaikin with the title: "21" Six grid-spanning 21 letter answers in that one, but what was truly amazing is that the clue for every one of those was... "21." Those answers: AGEFORDRINKINGLEGALLY NUMBERONEALBUMBYADELE GUNSINAMILITARYSALUTE SPOTSONALLSIDESOFADIE WINNINGBLACKJACKTOTAL LETTERSINTHESEANSWERS Wow. Wow. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=5/27/2018&g=92&d=D ...

A delightful and breezy debut puzzle. Hope we see more of you Leslie Benson. I only read the first several comments, and as usual a few of them are unnecessarily nasty. Please don't let those cranky people discourage you. 
I'm currently rereading the Harry Potter books and have read LOTR more than once as well, and I really enjoyed those references. Please keep making crosswords.

I only met Daniel Craig on two occasions but they were both memorable. The first time was at the Met Gala in ‘86. I was working as a bus boy and accidentally spilled heavy cream on the crotch of his trousers. He was very kind and didn’t make a fuss, despite the fact that he was no doubt wearing something one-of-a-kind and irreplaceable, and I always looked upon him charitably after that. The second time was years later in Rio. There is a restaurant and bar nested within one of the swimming pools at the Copacabana Palace and I was working there as a bus boy in the mid aughts.  Mr. Craig was having lunch with several guests and I accidentally spilled bananas foster on the crotch of his swimsuit. He was surprisingly relaxed about it and we all had a good laugh. Technically we had a third encounter on the moon last year (I was working part time there as a busboy), involving a custard stain on the inner leg of his spacesuit, but he refused to speak with me afterward, so I can’t rightly call it a meeting.
Congrats on the debut!
Great start to your crossword creating career. Thank you for a fun puzzle Leslie!

I don't know where else to write this, so here I am.  Please stop putting the Wordplay column on page 3 of the Sunday print edition!  Unless I remember that it's going to be there, I casually turn from the front page, and before I realize what I'm seeing, my puzzle solve has been tainted by clues.  I don't know who thinks this is a good idea, but I sure wish it would go away!
Another person with a Monday PB - 7:55. Also one of the few puzzles I can think of where I solved it in under 10 minutes. Pretty good, considering this is maybe my 3rd or 4th crossword this year!

This was an easy crossword that seemed hard. I didn't know a lot of the answers, which is unusual for a Monday, but before I knew it, the puzzle was solved. Everything I didn't know, I got through the crosses, and almost finished in record time (4 min). It was only after I finished that I could go back and appreciate the theme. Brava! Well done, and wonderful debut!

This has got to be the greatest compliment anybody has ever paid me. Thanks! And... https://www.theguardian.com/film/2026/feb/20/the-hunt-for-gollum-lord-of-the-rings-franchise?CMP=Share_AndroidApp_Other The necromancer rips open his robe and plunges the hungry, obsidian blade into his chest. Life force bursts out of him, splattering the nude bodies of his demonic assistants, the unholy altar and the pile of bones and dried skin laid out on it. "No!" cries the corpse of Gollum. "Let me be! Do not force me into the world again!" But it is too late. As the necromancer's unliving form drops to the cave's floor, Smeagol rises. He cries as studio execs put him in chains. "You're ours now" they say, and their mad cackles drown out the creature's wails.

commentBody:
@Matt

The ghosts of the priestesses of Ishtar stir in their dusty tombs beneath the sands of what was once their mistresses realm. They last sensed such a throbbing of blood in the before times, when chariots raced across the land in the shadow of ziggurats, bronze arrowheads chattering in the quivers of their bearded riders.

Is this the awakening? Has passion unfelt for millenia finally been released to burst out of the cracked earth of arid Canaan, letting Ishtar once more ride the wave of love and abandon, bringing us all sweet release?

Alas. It is only Matt reading a post on the NYT Crossword board.

@Andrzej 
I wouldn’t get all mopey about moped.

@Andrzej

That was awesome!

Thanks for taking us on a ride through the aspy wetness of your vocabulatorial adventure. 

How can one who hates the very word be so unstintingly delightful?!

@Matt

Maybe both of you.  Then it would be a two-stroke.

@Andrzej

Well, I was referring to a type of engine, more commonly found here on lawnmowers and mopeds.

commentBody:
@Heidi

It's what snorting powdered Oreos gets you.

@Heidi It’s the editors. Seems like we get some words that appear in relatively short spans of time and then go away again.

Poor performance on their part, IMO.
@Heidi The word STRIKE really unnerved me. Too close to current events.
@Nora The head honchos see what makes money and they go with it. It's capitalism.
@Heidi - Thank you! I thought I was the only one who noticed this phenomenon!

@Marshall Walthew It's called the Baader-Meinhof frequency illusion, although in your case it only happened once with that unusual word.  I used the word 'anathema' and a friend wasn't familiar. She said she heard it five times in the following week or so.

y that @Heidi

I was thinking the same thing yesterday. I saw a movie starring Andrew Garfield called "We Live in Time." It reminded me of another film I had seen recently. Both were about master chefs who wanted to leave their great mark on the world by making a fantastic dish--before dying of cancer. (I can't think of the name of the other film.)

I also recall a scene from "Grey's Anatomy" that was an almost exact duplicate of a scene from "Sex in the City."

The star has her wedding dress forcefully ripped from her back by a female friend when the nuptials go sour. What an image!!

I'd say it may be pure thievery.

Great story, Nora. Your dad is a great thinker. And, I love your "insular group" and "hive" references!

Along those lines, Jared Diamond's book Guns, Germs and Steel has a chapter titled "Necessity's Mother." He describes how ancient civilizations, separated by hundreds of years, huge land masses and oceans, have separately developed technology to suit their immediate needs. He cites innovations such as machinery, watercraft, weaponry, farming implements, barter systems, writings etc. All done completely free of knowledge that other civilizations even existed back then.

Bringing it to crossword puzzles, the constructors do seem to be unconsciously connected and their brain powers appear act in unison. Neuroscientist and musician Molly Gebrian writes about how our subconscious learns and somehow retains knowledge and develops muscle memory. Enough about that.

When Leslie wrote "quietly delighted that SMÉAGOL ended up in this grid," she didn't seem to specify that it was of her doing, nor did she elaborate on why she chose it.
My thought is that puzzles are submitted in an electronic form that allows scripting tools to access past cluing and solutions, much like xwordinfo provides. Possibly, the esteemed staff of geniuses at this publication, (completely unbiased, as they are (cough, cough)) publish the puzzles that contain similar ideas to piggyback on certain trends.

Great observation, Heidi, and equally great write-up!

Did you ever wonder how iconic movie quotes are spoken with close-up shots of the actors faces uttering those lines? I guess like "the money shot," there are "the money lines?" Take "smell of napalm, handle the truth, make my day, hell's coming with me, quick, pay me a compliment" etc. How did the producers/directors know those lines would become part of our vernacular? And how did they know to make the scene itself look so iconic so the consumers would always remember it?

As I mentioned in a reply to Nora, I believe the puzzles are submitted in an electronic form that allows scripting tools to access and compare past cluing and solutions, much like XWordinfo provides. This way, the editors can keep a trend going, start one or, as Barry/Bruce (same person) eluded to, if you read this publication, the clues make better sense. I don't read this pub, so completing the puzzles are more challenging for me.
Love your story!

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight

A tribe known for their unquestioning loyalty.  What use could we have for that word today?

How would the editors know that solvers would respond favorably to the first occurrence? And since when is Sméagol a trend? The last LOTR movie was released 20 years ago. So much to ponder.

I refuse to get my knickers in a twist over anything this trivial today.  The cat's on my bed and won't get up so I can make the bed.  I have to decide between going to a library book sale and pulling weeds in the garden, which are emitting guilt vibes, but which will be difficult as we've had no rain for weeks.  I'm out of coffee, and DHubby hasn't made a second potful.  It's Monday.

@Rebecca But then it wouldn't have been a Monday puzzle.

APRS and APYS would both work, but the [Indian flatbread] calls for the R. "Figs.] in the clue calls for a plural answer; you can compare APRS (or APYS). https://www.investopedia.com/personal-finance/apr-apy-bank-hopes-cant-tell-difference/

@JammieGirlCT

Something else to consider:

APRS and its singular, APR, have been clued as the financial abbreviation dozens of times.

APYS and its singular, APY, has never been in a NYT crossword puzzle.

The first pair are well known because of car commercials.

The other pair aren't well enough known to be considered for inclusion.

@Steve L: "APYS and its singular, APY, has never been in a NYT crossword puzzle."

AI Overview:
Based on a search of crossword database resources, the term APY (Annual Percentage Yield) has appeared in the New York Times crossword puzzle. 
It is generally used in the context of banking, finance, or interest rate clues, often appearing in the smaller, faster-paced puzzles like the NYT Mini Crossword.
It is often used as a 3-letter answer, fitting easily into the grid as part of themes related to banking or investment.

Steve,

@Just Another Rebecca You're misinformed.  You're never too old for Harry Potter.  As an adult you'll get many of the references children miss.  Read them, you'll like them.  Skip the movies.

@Tom Bantle

Totally agree! I was not the age range that would have been drawn to them at the time. Also, I was raised to believe they were  um evil...  

Anyhow, at the encouragement of a friend, I read them in my mid forties and fell in love! It was a time in my life when I had to give up on something I'd hoped and prayed for for years. I'd worked hard for and it was a tremendous sorrow to me...  the books were surprisingly so helpful in starting to heal. 

When I hit the Mirror of Erised in the first book and Dumbledore tells Harry, "It does not do to dwell on dreams and forget to live, remember that." It really hit me. It's exactly what I was doing. I knew I had to continue, and of course, they did nothing but get deeper and more meaningful over each book.🧙‍♂️⚡️🪄

Just Another Rebecca,
I assume your "Yes!" is directed to our guest columnist. If she is the crossword maven she says she is, not reading Harry Potter should not be a solving liability; everything she needs to know has been here many times.

I love seeing the quote that you put in your last paragraph. Thank you for that.

@HeathieJ also another great one - “Happiness can be found in the darkest of places, if only one remembers to turn on the light.” 

It reminds me to always stop and find a moment of light or happiness when I’m mentally struggling instead of ruminating in the negative.

@Captain Quahog I as watching this movie yesterday about Lake Michigan. The invasive bivalves--quagga mussels--in the lake are devouring all the plankton, so there's none left for juvenile whitefish. Quadrillions of bivalves. https://www.pbs.org/video/the-fight-to-save-lake-whitefish-zbytwf/

@lucky13 - Yes, they've been a problem for a while now. 

When they first appeared, some people hoped they would outcompete the zebra mussels. 

I haven't watched the video -- but I will. Did it mention that both zebra and quagga mussels have had some positive impact on highly eutrophic lakes? 

They're still a net negative, so please don't misconstrue my comment.
